speaker: interviewee
text: I had the order to come to Vilna and pick up the-- those snails, aluminum nails. And as they explained to me how it works, [INAUDIBLE] Vilna with a big wagon with lots of straw on top of load it. And-- because we used to make delivery of straw and hay. And we went to, and I went and got-- it was a-- was some house. This was in a big yard. It was-- everything was arranged. I came. We loaded the thing. We put the straw on top. And I went back to the [INAUDIBLE] work. And I was going on the-- it wasn't a highway at that time, but that road, from Vienna to-- going the main route, where all the convoys had been.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I made that turn to the side road, and very-- it was very narrow side road, and the edge-- and I-- I was in a hurry. So I made a sharp turn. And the whole wagon tip over. And convoys, you know, army trucks back and forth. And-- and some of them stop and want to help me. Now, they ask you, what happened? So I told him. And general said, this what happens? And I'm loading this-- and I can load it myself. They ask me if I can-- they can help me. Said no, thank you. You have more important things to do that-- to help me with the-- petty things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I loaded the-- the straw on top. But I was already home, so it was-- but I was thinking that this is the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they realize they would kill you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, I am telling that I'm having straw, and here I have cases, heavy cases, of-- not heavy cases, but case like [INAUDIBLE] none more-- nail cases. And-- and I came home. And we unloaded. And we use the nail. We are talking about riding. Once we had the order to deliver hay to Vilna ghetto. And nobody wants-- wanted to-- the regular people didn't wanted-- go to ghetto and-- and I said, if you don't want it-- if you are so shy, if you are so afraid of Jews, I will drive. I was always a-- trying to show a hero. And this was in winter, with a sled. And it was-- I took off, was dark. Maybe 4 o'clock-- 5 o'clock in the morning. I don't remember what time it was. It was very early. And-- and we had the order, because the only transportation in-- with the-- in the ghetto was horses. And actually, [INAUDIBLE] it was everything under German, Lithuanian [INAUDIBLE]. So we had the order to sell it. No, sell it-- wagon of hay to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they already made the arrangement first that say you got the money and-- and it's supposed to be delivered. And it was with this-- with the rope. And I went. But I took a big, big fur coat because it was cold and with the hood-- hood, so-- so one, nobody would recognize me. And was so-called when-- when you breathe, it was all white here. I used to wear a mustache, big mustache, for-- as a disguise.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- so I had to-- the [INAUDIBLE] I had to break it off from the mustache. And I came-- and I had also some bread and some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] swine meat, and-- [INAUDIBLE] fat. And bacon, raw bacon. And I happened to lose some bread on the way. But I noticed is that people and kids, ever there was running after me, because they was thinking. And I took the rope-- the-- the-- not the rope. The whip. And I make a noise that I will whip 'em out. And from then, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. As purpose-- yes, some-- I stole the bread. And-- and unload the thing. And I saw [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And a person, the most intelligent person, the most intelligent-- the person who know the language perfect, no photograph can express the feeling of the people. Things like this, you-- you don't forget those things. There were no people. They were most beggars. The kids were just wrecks. And naturally some people had been better off. But the majority-- I'm talking majority-- nobody-- you didn't see a laugh. You didn't see a loud voice. Like in-- on a funeral. Yeah, you see a funeral-- people in the funeral [INAUDIBLE] behave. Some people dragging feet, some people walking. Kids-- you know, you cannot describe. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you have this memory of this day in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody can describe what happened the ghetto. We are reading-- we are reading. We are seeing photographs. But this peanuts what-- what those people went through. This is the-- one-- only time I was in the ghetto. The reason I mention because-- and coming back later, we had the other orders to destroy the convoys. At that time, we had only armament. We been coming. And only trying to work from the woods. Later they the order to cut or-- I don't remember how many meters on each side of the road. Everything has to be leveled off. So-- and I was wounded only twice. Once in the hand here, still shows. If I would have my hand once in the [INAUDIBLE] to [INAUDIBLE], was in, because the help wasn't help. I was-- bleed to death, infection. Also, if I had one scratch, if I would bend myself one inch further-- [SOB] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was another time that you were saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times we had-- to-- to-- the best weapon was grenades. And we made-- we-- we-- and we-- we didn't made. We got from, you know, let's say call it a Molotov cocktail. Those are-- we used those, which was very hard to do, because we had to come very close where there were Molotov cocktails. Grenades was the best, but we always run-- the action was very short. And we run immediately to the woods. Because woods was only-- only the protection. They never chase us to the woods. Because they've been afraid somebody on a tree, somebody behind a tree, and always during the night. We never operate during the day. We couldn't operate during the day, always during the night. And they couldn't do nothing about it. But we did our job. And-- but the May-- when-- 1944, in-- when they started with the [INAUDIBLE], at that time, coming, for example, to the villagers and of the road to different places, to confiscate horses, buggies, and all-- all the transportation, and for food, they never went back to the back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been waiting for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- at that time, we already went in the uniforms. And the same thing was there with the road. They come-- they-- the armament in-- I think we were [INAUDIBLE] more fresh. And when they least expect it, we have been destroying the armament. And after that, we lost so also some people. But-- but they were not in this fighting spirit, that-- that-- [INAUDIBLE] in the hiding spirit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. At that time, but it looks like we didn't-- might-- might damage. But later, we'll find out that it was quite a bit there, because then didn't expect it, those things. And many, many things I found out when the convoys from those later came in-- different because they change those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because-- because a few.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Oh, I didn't tell you also. We had a-- over-- I-- I was invited quite often over there to them. And I knew exactly what's going on. And-- and we need some supplies, ammunition. And the best thing was, I-- I never saw blankets. Such heavy blankets, light like a feather. And we need those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one night, I took a horse, two horses, and two guys with me. And we came in we [INAUDIBLE] the barracks. And lots of details to tell you how we did it. And next morning, they came hunting. But in a very bad mood, you saw that something bothering them. And I said, are you sick or something bothers you, sir? Did you hear? I said, no, what did I hear? So they said some Communist came and exactly what we did. I started laughing. I said, you are talking joke, with all the people that you have-- you have a dog. You have a fence. You have a guide with a rifle all the time, walking back and forth, 24 hours. Impossible. You are telling me stories, no? They are telling me exactly what happened and there we took, exactly. They knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said, it must be something who was work-- from people who are-- because civilian had been working over there. And knew some Bolshevik Communist, he knew exactly what-- where what they have. But I still didn't believe them. [INAUDIBLE] say and think [INAUDIBLE]. And that time we really got good stuff. And this what we wanted. Yeah. Oh, I also was wearing always a-- whatever shirt I was wearing always with two pockets. Because in one pocket, I had one pill-- cyanide pill. And another cyanide. These were enclosed. So in case something happened, take a cyanide pill because the generals would promise you everything if you would only tell what the-- but the end was the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: They would kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing with other people. And they'll torture sometimes so much that a person cannot withdraw. So different-- so I was thinking, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I will die anyway if there will be-- if they [INAUDIBLE] suspicion, it was no end. It was no less--

speaker: interviewer
text: No solution.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever your excuse is, the only problem is they will give you death. And if you tell them, [INAUDIBLE]. And after you tell them, they will shoot you anyway. So why to go through all the torture? And I got this also from Vilna. [INAUDIBLE] was wrapped in such gray, like from a sack, material. And the reason why too is so quickly, because they would see--

speaker: interviewer
text: You can swallow.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you have your hands up or something, and you-- if you have to put the hands up, and you're already [INAUDIBLE]. And sometimes being caught, you know, and they-- you can never tell. When you went on any action, you never knew if you would come back and what would happen to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1944, and we had always [? Sam ?] or [? Derek ?] was [INAUDIBLE] from Vilna what to do. We never did anything on our own. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The forester is also-- sometime orders came to him because he could walk, you know. He was-- if I would go someplace, where did you go? I always was standing that I was going to a German [INAUDIBLE] in Vilna, because it's true. Because they had stables. And one time, for example, they'd been selling their unable horses. But only people who could get who-- who had some connection, and I was going. And I was going to pick up the horse for the farm. Or-- or-- how do you call it there? Yes. For the mouth of the horse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or for different things, leather things and, which was not-- not good anymore for army use, defective, [INAUDIBLE]. So I was always going get that, and I could-- was bringing to. And I was always [INAUDIBLE] so I had to bring or [INAUDIBLE]. Also I was coming to Vilna. I had friends which I was bringing them-- no Jewish friends. This is the family which told me their address. And I help him very much during that Russian time. This is a long story also. And I was bringing him a sack of cabbage, vegetables from the farm, which they allowed me to take it because I said I'm taking to my friends. And as I told you, 1944 in June was the end. In that time, I want to go-- the-- the-- the underground had an order to go to-- towards Warsaw and to defend Warsaw. But I didn't go to Warsaw. I want to go west to-- to home, to see my friends. But took a long time until this part of west Poland there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this was by the German border, by Poland, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the war end for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, ended in--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said in June?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, then I was on the Russian regime in June 1944. This [INAUDIBLE] they-- when the Russian liberated the territory. And this one, we had quite a big action with the-- oh, we have also, the German-- the Russian made arrangements that we could work on this side by side against German with them, with the Russian army. Then the high levels [INAUDIBLE]. But what happen when they came in the territory? The only allowed that we could join the Communistic underground, the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The other one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Which were the [INAUDIBLE] army. And actually, we didn't know all that. I-- for me, it wouldn't make a difference at that time. But, [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and as they arrested us, and put in the-- there's a long story about-- the end was that we [INAUDIBLE] big fence. And we-- we supposed to be sent out to Russia. This [INAUDIBLE] and one [INAUDIBLE] that it was in Russian. And that you say, they send people to Russia all the time. But, as [INAUDIBLE] was Warsaw, they didn't have much. And I notice that, in the evening, this on open field. They always had me getting food, soup-- what-- soup was whatever-- water, they had water with some-- a few potatoes. And the whole kettle was a few potatoes and a bread, piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ate it. And everybody was running to the-- nobody was around it. Everybody was running. And I think, this would be a good time to escape. So I took the two fellows. And dig out during the night in the corner. We were laying always in in the corner, the-- a tunnel. And with bare hands, and fortunately, the soil was so that was easy to-- but has to be big-- big enough so you could-- Go under.

speaker: interviewee
text: --go in there-- under. And I told them-- we dig this two-- two nights. Yeah. I saw a barn over there, and-- far away. Some farmer had a old barn. And I told them, when-- when-- [INAUDIBLE] to go for the food, we'll run here. And we'll run to the farm. I will run first. If everything is [INAUDIBLE] clear, and they don't catch me, you run after me. If they will catch me, they'll catch me. I said, no-- nothing to lose. And-- and I run. I saw nobody around. Done. And they run after me. We run to-- into the farm. And the farm, first of all, we have to hide. Normally, everybody would hide under straw, under hay because-- but I was thinking-- I saw a-- [INAUDIBLE], where they have for winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barn, they kept potatoes. Because so it wouldn't freeze it. So-- so I said, we run here. And it was a long, long under-- underground. And [INAUDIBLE] there's a corridor. And said, we hide here. And I told him, whatever will happen not to squeal a word. And was for quite a while. We had been sitting. Maybe it was 20 minutes, a half an hour, quite-- we heard a voice-- running voices, sound like a soldier, because-- because hear from the boats. And they-- they talked to each other Russian and said, yeah. They hide here in this barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came to the barn. They start screaming to us in Russian. You better get out, because we'll shoot you if you're not. If you-- if you get out, we'll let you go. No answer. So said, we know where you are hiding, in the hay. They [INAUDIBLE] because people would be sleeping on the hay also.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't go out. So they said, now we'll kill you with the bayonets. So the-- with bayonets, they poked it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They hay.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the side. [INAUDIBLE]. They went to the straw. Finally, they said, no. They couldn't be here. It was asked-- also asked, if you were-- if you don't get, we'll put the barn on fire. And you will perish. And I knew that they would not put the fire, because they are not against peasants. The whole population would be-- how could they burn for-- for three people? Finally they went out. We still didn't get out. I told them to keep it quiet. Because I was waiting for the evening. This was after [INAUDIBLE] close to the evening. I don't remember how long was it. I could hear the door open quiet. And between them and-- and they talking to themself in a whisper, that I knew already. They must be Russian [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and quickly they run into the barn. And the same [INAUDIBLE], we know they are here. You better get out. They were thinking they would surprise us. And I think for [INAUDIBLE], they went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was thinking they would come more, you know, trying to-- or-- or surrounded us. But after a while, the farmer, the owner, came with a-- tool wagon. He push himself. And I went to see, because I didn't hear. When I knew it was only one man, I could hear the wagon. So I stick my head to see what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the farmer. And he was taking hay for-- for the night to feed the animals, the-- to another barn. And so I jumped out, put the hands on his mouth, and said, I-- I'm not a Russian. I'm your friend. I'm not German. And because I was wearing a uniform, [INAUDIBLE] without any epaulettes and insignia. And I said, the only thing I want from you are some older eggs, whatever you have at home, and I will leave you my blanket, my good blanket, and all this good stuff, what we have in this sack. We had [INAUDIBLE] standing, the watches, the ration, and whatever we had-- somebody has a ring. And actually all the weapon.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- didn't take those. So we had those. And-- and I said, if you tell somebody that we are here, this barn will go on fire. And he was scared. Fire? This is livelihood.

speaker: interviewee
text: And bring us, and we'll go away. And you will never-- never see us again. And nobody knows-- knew that we are here. And so I'm quiet while he doesn't come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was thinking he said he will never get. But I was waiting for the night. And after a while, the door-- but from the other side, the barn from other side. And I could hear as it, but I still-- and he said, where are you? You know, because [INAUDIBLE] all the time. So I stick out my head, saw that the only one. And the first thing I said to him, if he told somebody, because I was afraid that the Russian soldier would be-- or he told his wife or somebody. No, no, he hadn't told-- I had to steal the [INAUDIBLE] and the-- and he brought the eggs. So we quickly change-- we gave him-- no, no. We told him that we will leave our clothes in here. And we quickly change. We left all our clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We took only a sack, and that's what we have-- almost empty sack. And when it was night, we run out. We knew where is the-- which direction is the highway. It wasn't highway, was a-- today we call it highway. It was a road to Vilna. And I went through those direction. And I could hear from a long distance the convoys, the Russian convoys, going towards-- trucks and other. And we went to the road. It was dark. But we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stepped across.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except nothing-- nothing-- but was already-- already was morning. Was start of the morning, so we-- they could see us. And finally [INAUDIBLE] stopped. And I tell him what we wanted. And they said they're not allowed to take us. Finally one stop. And I told him-- said to-- we came from a German camp. We have been working hard. We are almost dead. We want to come home. And we peasant. You know, we look like peasant, because we look like-- the rags, with the sack on the back, like-- like sack of potatoes. It was a sack, you know, almost nothing in it. And we are hungry. We have to go. And dirty, [INAUDIBLE]. We are dirty from the-- from the [INAUDIBLE] with-- sand was falling down. And-- and-- and finally, one-- but one said, we cannot take you in town. Because we are not allowed. But before the town was OK. And he-- [INAUDIBLE] we have been telling them on the road how the-- how hard we had to work for the Germans. And they beat us, and we are all-- almost that. And finally we-- they unload us before the town. And we walk. And all the-- and many people were-- been coming like us to town. And we came to their mother. The only thing I could come to the-- to the place where they live before, because they were from Vienna. And their father was an anti-Semite, anti-Semite, which I told you. But Mother was very delicate person. And their mother, I know, because she was coming to farm once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they knock on the door. And a mother said, who was that? And they said-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So she look, look. And she was afraid. Because she didn't recognize-- but they told her [CRYING]. It was them.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] And she thanked me, said I-- and finally we came. The first thing, we wash ourself. And I didn't know what-- what kind of-- and she took outside and burned the clothes. And I didn't have any clothes, but they from the father-- so, so I used his clothes. And they had at home [INAUDIBLE]. And later I had to do something about it. So it's a long story, with the-- how I came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I Want to ask you to conclude our interview, if you have a message to those who watch your interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I didn't finish interviews. There are many, many details to say and many things which I forgot to say it, while I was saying. But the-- people, when they say-- hear my stories, they think it's a nice story. It's not the story. 24 hours, I was watching if somebody would come for me. Whatever I did, it was always under tension. People don't understand how we fell, especially everybody saying, oh, you were on the Aryan papers. I wasn't Aryan papers-- I was a Jew, pretending to be Aryan and pretending was worse than being a Jew. Was-- during the night when I hear something, some noise, I would think, oh, they are coming for me. During the day, somebody said something like this. For example, when I was in the army, one officer said, oh, we have [INAUDIBLE]. We have a mess. When we are on the woods, also already, and open when we were in the uniforms. We have to make-- order is to make an altar, because it was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I told him, how do you make altar? You don't have a cross. You don't have nothing here. What do you mean? You are a Jew, is that you know how to make altar? You take wood. You make a cross. So I told him, if you wouldn't be in your uniform, I would punch him right in the nose. But I cannot [INAUDIBLE] for abuse, for-- Mhm. And this is how you handled this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because I-- I couldn't-- and then-- any other way. I was offended. I felt offended that he called me a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said if you wouldn't be in a uniform, I would punch him right in those nose. But I have respect for their uniform. I cannot do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. I want to thank you very much for your interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
